text_blocks.snippet: To treat a minor (first-degree) burn, immediately cool the area with cool—not cold—running water for 10 to 15 minutes. Gently remove jewelry or tight clothing, apply a thin layer of petroleum jelly, and cover it with a sterile, nonstick bandage. Take over-the-counter pain medication as needed.
snippet_highlighted_words: immediately cool the area with cool—not cold—running water for 10 to 15 minutes. Gently remove jewelry or tight clothing, apply a thin layer of petroleum jelly, and cover it with a sterile, nonstick bandage. Take over-the-counter pain medication as needed

text_blocks.snippet: For complete care instructions, follow these steps and precautions:

list.snippet: Stop the burning process: Move away from the heat source.
Remove tight items: Gently take off rings, watches, or tight clothing before swelling sets in.
Apply moisturizer: After cooling, you can apply petroleum jelly or aloe vera.
NO Ice: Extreme cold from ice or ice water can cause further tissue damage.
NO Home Remedies: Do not put butter, toothpaste, oils, or powders on the burn, as these trap heat and can cause infection.
NO Sticking: Do not pull off any clothing that is stuck to the burned skin.

text_blocks.snippet: Consult a doctor or visit an urgent care facility if the burn is larger than the palm of your hand, is located on the face, hands, feet, or groin, or if you notice signs of infection (such as pus, spreading redness, or fever).
For detailed first-aid guidelines, refer to the Mayo Clinic Burn First Aid Guide or the American Burn Association.
